item.title: Quick check: classify the thesis

item.note: No penalty. Make your call, then the video shows the situated version.

options.text: It names the larger question (how a society rations a necessity it can no longer fully supply) and still commits to protecting food first within that frame.
options.why: The reveal names both moves: name the larger question, then answer within it by committing to a position inside that frame.

options.text: It names the larger question about rationing and then stays neutral, leaving the food-first commitment out.
options.why: The reveal's move two is to commit to a position inside the frame; naming the question without committing would be the trap.

item.stem: When you rebuild the water claim into a situated thesis, which move set is what makes it situated rather than narrow?
item.title: Quick check: what makes it situated

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video gives the verdict.

options.text: The commit question: it names a larger question but never commits to an answer on the actual prompt, which is the trap.
options.why: The reveal says the claim names a larger question but never commits to an answer, so it fails the third question and is the trap.

options.text: The naming question: it never names a larger question inside the claim.
options.why: The reveal confirms the claim does name a larger question; the failure is the missing commitment, not a missing question.

item.stem: Run the three questions on "Every society must eventually decide how to ration scarce necessities." Which question does it fail?
item.title: Quick check: which question it fails

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: No. It names nothing; a transition word did the work and no cost was admitted, so it is a formula.
options.why: The reveal's verdict is that it names nothing and is a formula, because a transition word carried the sentence and no cost was admitted.

options.text: Yes. Admitting that people disagree is the cost the writer's side pays.
options.why: The reveal says disagreement is not a cost; only naming what choosing training actually costs would count.

item.stem: In the first try, does "some people disagree" name a real cost the writer's own side pays?
item.title: Quick check: real cost or formula

item.note: No penalty. Make your call, then the video shows the final version.

options.text: Name the specific cost (the same dollar cannot also protect the displaced worker), then let that cost shape the claim so the position holds only if it admits the choice.
options.why: The reveal names both moves: name the specific cost, then let that cost shape what the claim is willing to defend.

options.text: Add a transition like "on the other hand" to signal that the other side has a point.
options.why: The reveal treats a transition alone as naming no cost, which leaves only a formula, not a real tension.

item.stem: When you sharpen the training claim into a real tension, which move set is what makes it work?
item.title: Quick check: what makes the tension real

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms the check.

options.text: Names a specific cost the writer's own side pays and lets that cost shape the claim itself.
options.why: The reveal says to name the cost your side pays first, then let that cost shape the position.

options.text: Signals disagreement with a transition and then reaffirms the writer's side.
options.why: The reveal calls that a formula rather than a real tension, so it does not pass the check.

item.stem: A claim passes the real-tension check only if it does what?
item.title: Quick check: the real-tension test

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: weak
options.why: The reveal says the flattening word is weak: the power side gets called weak and dropped, leaving only one truth standing.

options.text: basic (calling food the more basic need)
options.why: The reveal shows a draft can call food the more basic need and still keep both truths in view, so ranking with basic does not flatten the tension.

item.stem: Check question one asks whether both truths are still live. Which word in the first try flattens the tension by dismissing one side?
item.title: Quick check: the flattening word

item.note: No penalty. Make your call, then the video shows the final version.

options.text: Reason from the clash (protecting food already requires protecting some power, one system not rivals) and land on a rule: a shared rationing rule beats naming a winner.
options.why: The reveal's moves are to grant the other truth, reason from the conflict, then land on a shared rationing rule.

options.text: Keep both truths in view and then rank food above power as the winner.
options.why: The reveal says the two are not rivals to rank; a shared rationing rule beats naming a winner.

item.stem: To pass check question two, you rewrite the second try so it argues from the conflict. Which move makes it work?
item.title: Quick check: reason from the clash

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: The point is that a budget has to choose (the hard choice); "training helps the next generation" is the minor idea to tuck into an "although."
options.why: The reveal subordinates the training clause with although and identifies the hard choice as the point that should land at the end.

options.text: The point is that training helps the next generation; the hard choice is the minor idea.
options.why: The reveal subordinates the training clause under although and treats the hard choice as the point, so this reverses the two.

item.stem: Which clause is the point the reader should remember (the one to place at the emphatic end), and which is the minor idea to subordinate?
item.title: Quick check: minor idea vs. the point

item.note: No penalty. Make your call, then the video shows one shaped version.

options.text: Subordinate the setting and style under "although" and let the point (the ending breaks the reader in two, and that is why the book stays with you) land last.
options.why: The reveal subordinates setting and style and places the point last, which is the whole shaping move.

options.text: Keep the clauses joined with "and" at equal weight but move the ending clause to the front.
options.why: The reveal treats equal-weight "and" chains as the flat problem; the fix subordinates the minor ideas and moves the point to the end, not the front.

item.stem: You reshape "The novel is set in a small village, and the prose is quiet, and the ending breaks the reader in two, and that is why the book stays with you." Which move makes it work?
item.title: Quick check: reshape the flat sentence

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: It fails the reason question: no reason is attached.
options.why: The reveal says it is a bare opinion with no reason attached, so it fails the reason question.

options.text: It fails the side question: it takes no side.
options.why: The reveal says it does take a side of sorts, so the side question is not the one it fails.

item.stem: The sentence "Homework is boring" is run through the three arguable-claim questions. Which question does it fail?
item.title: Quick check: which test does it fail?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Add a side someone could disagree with, and attach a reason.
options.why: The reveal rebuilds it by adding a side and a reason, so someone could disagree, and that is what makes it arguable.

options.text: Add more detail about how often students use phones.
options.why: The reveal shows extra detail alone does not help; the claim needs a side plus a reason to be arguable.

item.stem: You start with the fact "Students use phones during the school day." What two moves turn it into an arguable claim?
item.title: Quick check: what makes it arguable?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: One that names an exact amount, gives a real reason, and says why it matters.
options.why: The reveal keeps the version with an exact amount, a real reason, and stakes a reader can care about.

options.text: One that just repeats the idea more forcefully with words like definitely.
options.why: The reveal shows the weak try lacked an amount, a reason, and stakes, so louder wording is not the fix.

item.stem: You rewrite "Schools should pay students for good grades." Which rewrite is the strong one the writer landed on?
item.title: Quick check: the sharp rewrite

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Cap homework at one hour a night, with a reason, and stakes about students losing sleep.
options.why: The reveal says a passing version caps homework at one hour a night with stakes about losing sleep.

options.text: Homework is very bad and unfair to students.
options.why: The reveal requires a specific side and a so-what; this names no amount and no stakes, so it fails.

item.stem: You rewrite "Homework is bad." Which version passes the three questions: specific side, a reason, and a so-what?
item.title: Quick check: does it pass?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: No, it takes a side, which is an argue move, not explain.
options.why: The reveal says the first try took a side (a claim that volcanoes deserve attention), which is the wrong product for explain.

options.text: Yes, it matches explain.
options.why: The reveal says the first try argues instead of explaining, so it does not match the verb explain.

item.stem: The task says explain how a volcano erupts. Does the writer's first-try sentence match the verb explain?
item.title: Quick check: does it match EXPLAIN?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: The draft that takes a side and gives a reason.
options.why: The reveal says the answer is B because it takes a side and gives a reason, making it the arguable claim.

options.text: The neutral draft that has a focus but no side.
options.why: The reveal says the neutral draft is a controlling idea, the wrong product for an argue task.

item.stem: The verb is argue: should your school adopt a later start time? Which draft, A or B, matches argue?
item.title: Quick check: which draft argues?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Quote it.
options.why: The reveal says the answer is quote, because the exact wording and figure matter, so you keep them word for word.

options.text: Paraphrase it.
options.why: The reveal says the exact figure matters and must be kept word for word, so paraphrasing would lose it.

options.text: Summarize it.
options.why: The reveal says to keep the exact figure, so summarizing (which shortens and rewords) is the wrong move.

item.stem: A source gives an exact, striking figure (four point eight billion lunches) and the exact number matters. Should you quote, paraphrase, or summarize?
item.title: Quick check: which move fits?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: It names no source, so it is a dropped quote.
options.why: The reveal says no source was named, so it is a dropped quote, fixed by adding an attributive tag.

options.text: It is missing the exact figure.
options.why: The reveal keeps the figure in the fix and only adds the source, so the figure is not what is missing.

item.stem: Look at "The program served more than four point eight billion lunches." What is missing?
item.title: Quick check: what is missing?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: It must name its source, because no quote stands alone.
options.why: The reveal says to always name who said it with an attributive tag, since no quote stands alone.

options.text: A striking figure can stand on its own without naming a source.
options.why: The reveal says no quote stands alone and you must never let a fact walk in as a stranger.

item.stem: You run the three-question check before submitting (named the source, chose the move on purpose, fact is really in the source). What must be true for the sentence to be ready?

options.text: No, it is off-point: it tells the path, not the reason.
options.why: The reveal says the flyways fact is about the path birds take, not the reason, so it is off-point.

options.text: Yes, it proves why birds migrate.
options.why: The reveal says the flyways fact is off-point and does not give the reason birds migrate.

item.stem: Does the fact "Birds follow set routes called flyways" prove why birds migrate (to survive the seasons)?
item.title: Quick check: does it prove it?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: It matches what the claim actually says and names its source.
options.why: The reveal says to match the fact to what the claim says and name the source before you commit.

options.text: It is the first true fact you found in the passage.
options.why: The reveal warns against the trap of grabbing the first fact you find, since it may not be on-point.

item.stem: You run the relevance test on the according-to-the-passage fact. What makes a fact good proof?

options.text: The one that is integrated into a built sentence.
options.why: The reveal says B is integrated, while A leaves you guessing how the number connects.

options.text: The one that leaves the reader to figure out how the number connects.
options.why: The reveal says A leaves the reader to work out how the number connects, so it is not integrated.

item.stem: Two sentences use the same quote (forty-eight minutes). Which one, A or B, folds it into a built sentence?

options.text: One built sentence that names Pew and folds the seventy-two percent inside it.
options.why: The reveal keeps a version where the quote is folded into a built sentence that names Pew and flows so the reader sees the point.

options.text: The number sits alone, then Pew is named in a separate sentence.
options.why: The reveal shows a number parked by itself, even with the source named separately, still fails.

item.stem: You fold the quote (seventy-two percent) from Pew into one sentence. Which version passes the check?
item.title: Quick check: which sentence passes?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Because.
options.why: The reveal uses because, which states the reason the fact supports the claim.

options.text: But.
options.why: The reveal shows the fact backs the claim rather than limiting it, so but does not fit.

options.text: So.
options.why: The reveal links the fact as the reason for the claim, not as a result of it, so so does not fit.

item.stem: Claim: schools should offer free meals to all students. Fact: the program already reaches millions who would go hungry. Which hinge links them: because, but, or so?
item.title: Quick check: pick the hinge

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: But.
options.why: The reveal uses but, because the fact names a limit on the claim.

options.text: Because.
options.why: The reveal says the fact names a limit, not a reason for the claim, so because does not fit.

options.text: So.
options.why: The reveal says the fact pushes against the claim, not follows from it, so so does not fit.

item.stem: Claim: The program is large. Fact: It still misses some families. Which hinge shows the relationship: because, but, or so?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: A restatement.
options.why: The reveal says the because carries no reason at all; it just repeats the claim.

options.text: A real warrant.
options.why: The reveal says a real warrant gives a reason that links the fact to the claim, which this sentence does not.

item.stem: Is "Schools should require service because it really should be required" a real warrant or a restatement?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Yes, it passes all three.
options.why: The reveal says because is the causal word, it explains why instead of restating, and it uses the fact that most high schools already run programs.

options.text: No, it only restates the claim.
options.why: The reveal says the sentence explains why instead of restating, so it does not fail as a restatement.

item.stem: You run the three warrant questions on the sentence (causal word, explains why, uses a fact from the source). Does it pass?

options.text: Spell out the link: connect the moving-water loop to the meaning of reuse.
options.why: The reveal writes a warrant that connects the evaporating loop back to the word reuse, so all three parts hold together.

options.text: Restate that the water cycle is important.
options.why: The reveal shows a warrant explains the link, not repeats a claim, so restating does not work.

item.stem: You write a warrant for why evaporating and falling water proves the water cycle reuses the same water. What must the warrant do?

options.text: Point, then Evidence, then Warrant.
options.why: The reveal says to run the check in order: Point, then Evidence, then Warrant, or it will not hold together.

options.text: Evidence first, then a Point, then a summary.
options.why: The reveal says missing one or going out of order breaks the paragraph, so this order fails.

options.text: Pair A.
options.why: The reveal says Pair A adds up: one gives the usage rate, the other shows the scale of what sits unused.

options.text: Pair B.
options.why: The reveal says Pair B just repeats the same idea, so it does not add up.

item.stem: Point: our school library is underused. Pair A (about twelve percent borrowed a book; the library holds forty thousand books) or Pair B (twelve percent borrowed; very few checked anything out). Which pair adds up?
item.title: Quick check: which pair adds up?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: The second fact adds something the first needs (a baseline), and one warrant ties both to the point.
options.why: The reveal says the second fact was chosen because it adds the baseline the first fact needs, with one warrant tying both.

options.text: The two facts repeat the same idea for emphasis.
options.why: The reveal says facts that just repeat (like Pair B) do not develop the point.

options.text: No, it is a neutral fact both sides accept.
options.why: The reveal says both sides would nod at it, and a fact both sides accept is not a counterargument.

options.text: Yes, it is a real counterargument.
options.why: The reveal says it does not push back on the claim, so it does not oppose it.

item.stem: Claim: schools should require community service. First try: "Many students already volunteer on their own." Is that a real counterargument?
item.title: Quick check: real counterargument?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: The writer's own side in disguise.
options.why: The reveal says it is the writer's own claim again, just dressed up with a reason, not the other side.

options.text: The other side speaking.
options.why: The reveal says it is not the other side at all, so it does not oppose the writer.

item.stem: Second try: "Community service teaches responsibility, so schools should require it." Is that the other side, or the writer's own side in disguise?
item.title: Quick check: whose side is it?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: It opposes your claim, is what the other side would really say, and is their strongest form.
options.why: The reveal says a real counterargument opposes your claim, is something the other side would really say, and is their strongest point.

options.text: It is a fact both sides accept, or a weak point easy to knock down.
options.why: The reveal rules out facts both sides accept and weak caricatures you could knock down.

item.stem: You write the strongest point the other side would make about banning phones. What makes a counterargument real?
item.title: Quick check: what makes it real?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: It does not concede the other side's strongest objection, the cost.
options.why: The reveal says the first try did not concede; it skipped the other side's strongest objection, the cost.

options.text: It is missing a reason for its own side.
options.why: The reveal shows the first try does give a reason (free rides get people to work), so a reason is not what is missing.

item.stem: Claim: cities should make public transit free. First try: "Cities should make the buses free, because free rides get people to work." Which of the three moves is missing?
item.title: Quick check: which move is missing?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Concede a real objection, answer it with a reason, and still hold your position.
options.why: The reveal says to concede a real objection, answer with a reason, and still hold a clear position.

options.text: Concede the objection and then switch to the other side's position.
options.why: The reveal says conceding sets up your answer; it never replaces your position.

item.stem: You write one "Although X, Y because Z" sentence defending a later start time. What must it do?

options.text: It only names the worry, then moves on, so the worry is still standing.
options.why: The reveal says it only names the worry, then repeats the claim, so the money worry is still standing.

options.text: It answers the money worry with a reason.
options.why: The reveal says it repeats the claim instead of answering, so the worry is not answered.

item.stem: "Some say it wastes money. But free meals help students learn." Does this answer the money worry with a reason, or only name it and move on?
item.title: Quick check: does it answer it?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: State your position, name the other side fairly, and answer it with a reason.
options.why: The reveal names the three parts as position, the other side, and answer, with a reason.

options.text: State your position, name the other side, then move on.
options.why: The reveal says the common failure is naming the objection and then dropping it, which leaves the worry standing.

item.stem: You run the three-question check on the paragraph. What three parts must a counterargument paragraph have?
item.title: Quick check: the three parts

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: An argument claim.
options.why: The reveal says the words "most important" take a side another reader could dispute, so it is an argument claim.

options.text: A fact.
options.why: The reveal says the sentence takes a side, and a fact takes no side, so it is not a fact.

options.text: A controlling idea.
options.why: The reveal says it leans toward a side, but a controlling idea sets a neutral focus, so it is not one.

item.stem: Is "The water cycle is the most important process on Earth" a fact, an argument claim, or a controlling idea?

options.text: Does it name the parts it will cover?
options.why: The reveal says the sentence names no parts, so this is the one question it fails.

options.text: Does it set a focus?
options.why: The reveal says the focus question passes, so this is not the one it fails.

options.text: Does it stay neutral and take no side?
options.why: The reveal says the no-side question passes, so this is not the one it fails.

item.stem: Run the three controlling-idea questions on "The water cycle moves water around the planet." Which question does it fail?
item.title: Quick check: the one that fails

options.text: Sequence: they are steps in order.
options.why: The reveal says these are steps in order, which is a sequence, so you use sequence signposts.

options.text: Add: they are similar ideas.
options.why: The reveal says these are ordered steps, not simply similar ideas being added, so add does not fit.

item.stem: A draft moves from taking in sunlight, then water, then making sugar. What relationship connects these links?
item.title: Quick check: label the links

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Contrast: use However.
options.why: The reveal says hard studying and a low score pull against each other, so the fix uses However.

options.text: Add: keep Also.
options.why: The reveal says add does not fit because the ideas clash, so Also is wrong.

item.stem: Draft: "I studied for the quiz all week. Also, I got a low score." What relationship is hiding, and which signpost fits?
item.title: Quick check: which signpost?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: It could point to more than one thing (the long distance, the harsh weather, or both), so it is unclear.
options.why: The reveal lists the candidates as the long distance, the harsh weather, or both, showing This is unclear.

options.text: It clearly points to one thing already.
options.why: The reveal shows This has several possible meanings, so it does not point to one clear thing yet.

item.stem: In the first sentence, what is the problem with the word "This"?
item.title: Quick check: what is the problem?

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: Add an anchoring noun after This that names the real thing, like this combination of buzzing phones and piling homework.
options.why: The reveal fixes it with This combination of buzzing phones and piling homework, an anchoring noun that names the real thing.

options.text: Leave This alone but add an exclamation mark for emphasis.
options.why: The reveal says the fix is an anchoring noun that names the real thing, not louder punctuation.

item.stem: Rewrite "Phones keep buzzing during class, and homework keeps piling up at night. This is wearing me out." How do you fix the last sentence?
item.title: Quick check: how to fix it

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: way better
options.why: The reveal flags "way better" as a slang or casual word to cut.

options.text: is more effective
options.why: The reveal flags slang and casual wording, but a formal phrasing like this is not one of the markers.

item.stem: The video hunts for informal markers of three kinds (first person, slang, talking to the reader). Which phrase is an informal marker to cut?
item.title: Quick check: spot the marker

item.note: No penalty. Just your first call, then the video confirms it.

options.text: No first person, no slang, no talking to the reader; it states the position and gives a real reason.
options.why: The reveal says the strong rewrite has no first person, no slang, no reader address, states the position, and gives a real reason.

options.text: Keeps the position but still says you can totally see why.
options.why: The reveal says a formal claim cuts talking to the reader, so keeping that phrase fails.

item.stem: You rewrite a casual draft as a formal, objective claim. Which rewrite fits?
item.title: Quick check: which rewrite is formal?
